quote: Work for the food that endures for eternal life.
firstReading: In Acts 6:8-15 Stephen, filled with grace and power, performs great signs and wonders among the people. His opponents cannot withstand his wisdom or the Spirit by which he speaks, so they secretly instigate false witnesses. They accuse him of speaking blasphemous words against Moses and God, rousing the council and setting the stage for a public confrontation against him.
psalmSummary: Psalm 119:23-24, 26-27, 29-30 presents the psalmist’s trust in God’s instruction amid opposition. Though princes plot against the servant, the psalmist delights in God’s testimonies and takes them as counsel. He asks God to teach him and to keep him from deceitful paths, choosing the way of faithfulness and earnest meditation on the law as guidance for life.
gospelSummary: In John 6:22-29 the crowd finds Jesus after the feeding of the five thousand and questions him about signs and provision. Jesus warns them not to labor for perishable food but to seek the food that endures to eternal life, given by the Son of Man. When they ask what works God requires, Jesus answers plainly that the work of God is to believe in the One he has sent, calling for faith in God’s revelation rather than mere signs or displays.
saintReflection: St. Agnes of Montepulciano (Optional Memorial): St. Agnes of Montepulciano (1268-1317) was an Italian Dominican nun and mystic renowned for her profound holiness from a very young age, becoming a superior at just 15 years old. She was famous for her deep prayer life, miracles, and the physical manifestation of manna falling like snow upon her during her prayers. Her incorrupt body was later venerated by St. Catherine of Siena.
dailyPrayer: Lord Jesus, you invite us to seek food that endures for eternal life and to trust in the One the Father has sent. Give us hearts that long for you above passing satisfactions. Grant, like Stephen, courage to witness to your truth with wisdom and the Spirit, and like St. Agnes, a deep life of prayer that receives your heavenly nourishment. Guard us from wandering after mere signs; teach us to believe and to live by your word. Strengthen our faith so our lives become works of trust, love, and service to others, for your glory and the life of the world. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: The readings juxtapose signs, witness, and the demand of faith. Stephen’s ministry shows that divine power works through persons filled with the Spirit even amid opposition, while the psalm insists on meditation on God’s law as inner counsel. Jesus refrocuses popular expectation from spectacular signs to belief in the Sender as the authentic work God requires. Together they teach that true nourishment is relational—participation in the gift of the Father through the Son by the Spirit—so that works are rightly ordered to faith and sustained by God’s word rather than by mere human striving.
exegesis:
Context: The Acts passage follows the selection of the seven and recounts the early Church’s life as it grows amid both wonder and opposition. Stephen emerges as a prominent figure: empowered for ministry, effective in signs, and eloquent under trial. His opponents’ resort to false witnesses reveals how threatened institutional leaders become when the Spirit undermines established authority. The scene anticipates the pattern of prophetic confrontation and martyrdom that will mark the growing movement.

First Reading: Acts 6:8-15 presents Stephen as the exemplar of Spirit-led ministry. The narrative stresses two linked realities: the authenticity of Stephen’s ministry—shown by “great wonders and signs”—and the hostile reaction of those who cannot refute his wisdom. Their strategy is deception, inventing blasphemy to discredit him publicly. The passage demonstrates how truth can provoke entrenched powers and how the community’s gifts may be countered by false accusation, setting the stage for judicial spectacle rather than fair inquiry.

Psalm: The selected verses from Psalm 119 frame the believer’s posture amid opposition: meditation on God’s statutes, delight in his testimonies, and seeking instruction. The psalmist’s response to princes who plot is internal fidelity. Rather than retaliation, the remedy is reverent reflection and moral choice—asking God to remove deceitful ways and to teach the straight path. The psalm supplies spiritual resources for those like Stephen: a turn to God’s law as counselor and a disposition of trust under trial.

Gospel: John 6:22-29 moves the conversation from signs to substance. After the miraculous feeding, the crowd pursues Jesus seeking more signs and daily sustenance. Jesus reframes their pursuit: they should not seek perishable food but the bread that endures—food given by the Son of Man, which points ultimately to eternal life. When pressed about what works God requires, Jesus answers decisively: the work is to believe in the One the Father has sent. The Gospel thus shifts focus from external proof to personal trust in God’s revelation through Jesus.

Saints: St. Agnes of Montepulciano’s life resonates with these scriptural motifs. Her contemplative receptivity, reported miracles, and the sign of heavenly manna connect symbolically with the Johannine theme of divine nourishment. Agnes’s early maturity in leadership and holiness models how faith integrates prayer and action: prayer supplies the inner nourishment that empowers witness. Like Stephen she drew both admiration and opposition, yet remained rooted in God’s presence, a living testimony to the work God desires.

Today: For contemporary believers the readings call for discernment between immediate gratification and the deeper work God desires. The temptation to measure God by signs remains strong; yet Jesus calls for faith in the Sender. The psalm and the witness of the saints teach that such faith is cultivated through sustained meditation on God’s word and persistent prayer. Practically, Christians are invited to let belief shape their works—serving others not to earn favor but as response to God’s gift—and to remain steadfast when truth provokes misunderstanding. In a world of distraction, these texts reorient us to seek the lasting bread that sustains the soul and to bear witness with patience and courage.
